title: Myakka Wild and Scenic River Act of 2025
short_title: Myakka Wild and Scenic River Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Myakka Wild and Scenic River Act of 2025 adds a 34-mile section of the Myakka River in Sarasota County, Florida to a special national system that protects important rivers. The Secretary of the Interior will work with local people to manage and protect this river. Different parts of the river will be labeled as wild, scenic, or recreational depending on how natural they are and how much development is nearby. This law will help keep the Myakka River clean and beautiful for people to enjoy in the future.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
subjects: Wilderness and natural areas, wildlife refuges, wild rivers, habitats

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Myakka Wild and Scenic River Act of 2025.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts. In 1978, another law required a study of the Myakka River to see if it should be added to the national wild and scenic rivers system. This study found that the Myakka River should be included in the National Wild and Scenic Rivers System. The State of Florida has shown it wants to protect this river by passing the Myakka River Wild and Scenic Designation and Protection Act (Florida Statute Chapter 258.501). Sarasota County and the cities of Venice and North Port have shown they want to protect this river through their land use plans, local laws, and development rules. Many people and groups want this river to be part of the National Wild and Scenic Rivers System. This includes strong public support, state and local government support, and backing from the Myakka River Management Coordinating Council. This council represents many different state and local agencies, farming interests, landowners, environmental groups, and nonprofit organizations. A twelve mile section of the Myakka River runs through Myakka River State Park. Another twenty two mile section contains conservation lands that are owned by the public or protected by conservation easements (legal agreements that protect land) within the Myakka River Watershed.

sections.title: Section 3  Designation of Myakka River, Florida, as a Component of the National Wild and Scenic Rivers System
sections.text: This section changes the Wild and Scenic Rivers Act by adding a new paragraph at the end of Section 3(a). The new paragraph designates the Myakka River in Florida. It covers the thirty four mile segments within Sarasota County. The Secretary of the Interior will manage these segments working together with the Myakka River Management Coordinating Council. The river segments in Sarasota County will be managed in different classifications. The first segment is about eight miles long from the Manatee County and Sarasota County line to State Road 72. This will be classified as a scenic river. The second segment is about eleven and two tenths miles long from State Road 72 to Laurel Road. This will be classified as a wild river. The third segment is about one and nine tenths miles long from Laurel Road to Border Road. This will be classified as a scenic river. The fourth segment is about one and five tenths miles long from Border Road to just south of the Interstate 75 Bridge. This will be classified as a recreational river. The fifth segment is about one and five tenths miles long from south of the Interstate 75 Bridge to Snook Haven. This will be classified as a scenic river. The sixth segment is about three and two tenths miles long from Snook Haven to Ramblers Rest. This will be classified as a wild river. The seventh segment is about two and seven tenths miles long from Ramblers Rest to US Highway 41. This will be classified as a scenic river. The eighth segment is about four miles long from US Highway 41 to the Charlotte County Line. This will be classified as a scenic river.

sections.title: Section 4  Special Requirements Applicable to Myakka River
sections.text: This section provides definitions and special requirements. The term Myakka River means the segments of the Myakka River in Florida that are designated as part of the National Wild and Scenic Rivers System by this Act. The term Council means the Myakka River Management Coordinating Council established under Florida law Chapter 258.501. The term comprehensive management plan means the Myakka River Wild and Scenic Management Plan developed by the Council. This plan will meet the requirements for a comprehensive management plan under the Wild and Scenic Rivers Act. The term Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior. To provide long term protection and enhancement of the Myakka River, the Secretary will coordinate administration duties under this section. The Secretary may make cooperative agreements with the State of Florida Department of Environmental Protection Division of Recreation and Parks, local governments, Sarasota County, the City of North Port, the City of Venice, and local planning and nonprofit organizations. When the Secretary of the Interior manages the Myakka River through cooperative agreements, this will not make it part of the National Park Service administration or part of the National Park System. Nothing in this Act or any agreement will affect how publicly or privately owned lands within the Myakka River watershed are managed by the agencies that have authority over those lands. This management will follow Florida law 258.501 and the mission of those agencies. The Secretary may provide technical help, staff support, and funding to help update and carry out the comprehensive management plan. The Secretary can only acquire land for the Myakka River segments if someone donates the land or if the owner agrees to sell it. No land or rights to land may be taken for the Myakka River segments through condemnation (forced government purchase).

sections.title: Section 5  Myakka River Management Council
sections.text: The Secretary will work with the existing Council to update and carry out the comprehensive management plan for the Myakka River. The Secretary will choose a representative from the National Park Service to be added to the Council. Other interested groups may be added to the Council following Florida law 258.501. The Secretary will work with the Council to update and carry out the management plan for the Myakka River. This will help the Secretary fulfill the management duties required by this Act.
